news_summary: Today's environmental breakthroughs spotlight the vital role of protected natural sites, innovative conservation efforts restoring rare species, new insights into tree electrification during storms, and advanced solar technology achieving significant efficiency gains.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées environnementales d'aujourd'hui mettent en lumière le rôle essentiel des sites naturels protégés, les initiatives innovantes de conservation visant à réintroduire des espèces rares, les nouvelles découvertes concernant l'électrification des arbres lors des tempêtes, ainsi que les technologies solaires de pointe permettant d'atteindre des gains d'efficacité significatifs.
news_summary_es: Los avances medioambientales de hoy ponen de relieve el papel fundamental de los espacios naturales protegidos, las iniciativas innovadoras de conservación para la recuperación de especies raras, los nuevos conocimientos sobre la electrificación de los árboles durante las tormentas y la tecnología solar avanzada que permite lograr importantes mejoras en la eficiencia.

articles.title: Nature’s last strongholds: Why UNESCO sites are a lifeline for a planet in peril
articles.summary: In a world where the drumbeat of environmental loss is growing louder, a new landmark report from UNESCO has revealed a rare glimmer of hope. While global wildlife has plummeted by a staggering 73 per cent since the 1970s, life within the UN science and culture agency’s protected territories is holding its ground.
articles.content:
From the mist-covered peaks of Global Geoparks to the vibrant underwater cathedrals of World Heritage coral reefs, these sanctuaries are doing more than just preserving scenery; they are acting as the planet’s vital organs.

The report, titled People and Nature in UNESCO-Designated Sites, is the first of its kind to look at UNESCO’s entire network as a single, massive web of protection. Covering over 13 million square kilometres – an area larger than China and India combined – this network of 2,260 sites is proving that when we protect nature, nature protects us back.

© UNESCO/Tamara Merino A woman stands with her husband with a bundle of freshly sheared vicuña fibre in Chungará, Lauca National Park, Chile.

A fortress for biodiversity

The statistics are nothing short of breath-taking. These sites are home to more than 60 per cent of all mapped species on Earth. Even more crucially, four out of every ten species found within these borders exist nowhere else. If these habitats vanish, these creatures vanish forever.

Tweet URL

“The findings are clear: UNESCO sites are delivering for both people and nature,” says UNESCO Director-General Khaled El-Enany.

“Inside these territories, communities thrive, humanity’s heritage endures, and biodiversity is holding on while it collapses elsewhere.”

Beyond the animals and plants, these landscapes are silent giants in the fight against climate change. They store an estimated 240 gigatons of carbon – the equivalent of nearly 20 years of current global emissions.

If these ecosystems were destroyed, that carbon would be released back into the atmosphere, acting like a “carbon bomb” that would make climate goals impossible to reach.

The human heart of conservation

One of the most striking revelations of the report is that these are not empty wildernesses.

UNESCO sites are living, breathing landscapes home to nearly 900 million people – roughly one in ten people on the planet.

They are also bastions of human culture. Over 1,000 languages are documented across these territories, and a quarter of the sites overlap with Indigenous Peoples’ lands.

In regions like Africa and Latin America, that figure rises to nearly 50 per cent. The report makes it clear: you cannot protect the land without the people who have been its guardians for millennia.

Economically, the impact is just as significant. Approximately 10 per cent of global GDP is generated within or around these zones, proving that conservation and prosperity can go hand-in-hand.

© UNESCO/SNadim Kesserwani The Jabal Moussa Biosphere Reserve is located on the western slopes of the Mount Lebanon range and overlooking the Mediterranean sea.

A network under fire

However, the report carries a heavy warning. The “lifeline” is fraying. Nearly 90 per cent of these sites are facing intense environmental stress. In just ten years, climate-related hazards like fires and floods have jumped by 40 per cent.

Experts warn that by 2050, one in four UNESCO sites could hit a “tipping point.” This could see glaciers disappearing entirely, coral reefs collapsing into rubble, and lush forests drying out until they start releasing more carbon than they absorb.

© UNESCO/Ola Jennersten Straddling the Arctic Circle, the Vindelälven–Juhttátahkka Biosphere Reserve includes large parts of the Vindelfjällen nature reserve, one of the largest in Europe.

The path forward: A call to action

The good news? It is not too late. The report suggests that every single degree of warming we avoid could halve the number of sites facing total disruption by the end of the century.

UNESCO is now calling for a “scale-up” in global ambition, urging governments to move beyond seeing these sites merely as “pretty places” for tourists. Instead, they must be treated as strategic assets.

“This is an urgent call to recognise UNESCO sites as strategic assets in tackling climate change,” says El-Enany.

The strategy is simple but bold: restore damaged ecosystems, work across national borders to protect migrating wildlife, and – most importantly – ensure that Indigenous Peoples and local communities are the ones leading the way.

By investing in these sites today, we aren’t just saving a park or a monument; we are safeguarding the future of the planet itself, UNESCO says.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant positive environmental impact through UNESCO's protected sites, which preserve biodiversity, store vast carbon amounts, and support nearly 900 million people and Indigenous cultures. It provides detailed data on the scale and importance of these sites, their role in climate mitigation, and the urgent call for increased global action, making it a substantial and inspiring environmental story.
articles.personality_title: UNESCO sites protect wildlife, culture, and climate on a vast scale
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since the 1970s, global wildlife populations have dropped by 73%, and many natural places face threats from climate change and human activities. UNESCO, the United Nations agency for science and culture, protects special areas around the world to help nature and people survive.

**What happened** – UNESCO released a report studying 2,260 protected sites covering over 13 million square kilometers. These include parks, coral reefs, and cultural areas that shelter more than 60% of all known species, with 40% found nowhere else. Nearly 900 million people live in these areas, many of them Indigenous communities who have cared for the land for thousands of years.

**Impact** – These sites act like the planet’s lungs and heart by storing large amounts of carbon—enough to match 20 years of global emissions—and by protecting unique animals and plants. They also support local economies, generating about 10% of the world’s economy. The report shows that saving these places helps both nature and people.

**What's next step** – UNESCO warns that many sites face growing dangers like fires and floods, which could cause serious damage by 2050. The agency urges governments to treat these sites as important tools against climate change. Protecting and restoring these areas, working across countries, and involving Indigenous peoples in leadership are key to keeping these natural treasures safe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – UNESCO’s protected sites are crucial havens for wildlife, culture, and climate, but they need stronger global support to face rising environmental threats.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les sites UNESCO protègent la faune, la culture et le climat à grande échelle
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis les années 1970, les populations mondiales de faune ont chuté de 73 %, et de nombreux espaces naturels sont menacés par le changement climatique et les activités humaines. L’UNESCO, l’agence des Nations Unies pour la science et la culture, protège des zones spéciales dans le monde pour aider la nature et les populations à survivre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’UNESCO a publié un rapport étudiant 2 260 sites protégés couvrant plus de 13 millions de kilomètres carrés. Ces sites comprennent des parcs, des récifs coralliens et des zones culturelles qui abritent plus de 60 % de toutes les espèces connues, dont 40 % que l’on ne trouve nulle part ailleurs. Près de 900 millions de personnes vivent dans ces zones, dont beaucoup de communautés autochtones qui prennent soin de ces terres depuis des milliers d’années.

**Impact** – Ces sites agissent comme les poumons et le cœur de la planète en stockant de grandes quantités de carbone – assez pour correspondre à 20 ans d’émissions mondiales – et en protégeant des animaux et des plantes uniques. Ils soutiennent aussi les économies locales, générant environ 10 % de l’économie mondiale. Le rapport montre que préserver ces lieux aide à la fois la nature et les populations.

**Prochaine étape** – L’UNESCO avertit que de nombreux sites font face à des dangers croissants comme les incendies et les inondations, qui pourraient causer de graves dégâts d’ici 2050. L’agence encourage les gouvernements à considérer ces sites comme des outils importants contre le changement climatique. Protéger et restaurer ces zones, collaborer entre pays, et impliquer les peuples autochtones dans la gestion sont essentiels pour préserver ces trésors naturels.

**Une phrase clé** – Les sites protégés par l’UNESCO sont des refuges essentiels pour la faune, la culture et le climat, mais ils ont besoin d’un soutien mondial renforcé pour faire face aux menaces environnementales croissantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Los sitios UNESCO protegen la vida silvestre, la cultura y el clima a gran escala
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde la década de 1970, las poblaciones mundiales de vida silvestre han caído un 73%, y muchos lugares naturales enfrentan amenazas por el cambio climático y actividades humanas. UNESCO, la agencia de la ONU para la ciencia y la cultura, protege áreas especiales en todo el mundo para ayudar a la naturaleza y a las personas a sobrevivir.

**Qué pasó** – UNESCO publicó un informe que estudia 2,260 sitios protegidos que cubren más de 13 millones de kilómetros cuadrados. Estos incluyen parques, arrecifes de coral y áreas culturales que albergan más del 60% de todas las especies conocidas, con un 40% que no se encuentran en ningún otro lugar. Cerca de 900 millones de personas viven en estas áreas, muchas de ellas comunidades indígenas que han cuidado la tierra durante miles de años.

**Impacto** – Estos sitios funcionan como los pulmones y el corazón del planeta al almacenar grandes cantidades de carbono, suficiente para igualar 20 años de emisiones globales, y proteger animales y plantas únicas. También apoyan economías locales, generando alrededor del 10% de la economía mundial. El informe demuestra que salvar estos lugares ayuda tanto a la naturaleza como a las personas.

**Próximo paso** – UNESCO advierte que muchos sitios enfrentan peligros crecientes como incendios e inundaciones, que podrían causar daños graves para 2050. La agencia insta a los gobiernos a tratar estos sitios como herramientas clave contra el cambio climático. Proteger y restaurar estas áreas, trabajar entre países y asegurar que los pueblos indígenas lideren son pasos esenciales para mantener estos tesoros naturales a salvo.

**Frase clave** – Los sitios protegidos por UNESCO son refugios vitales para la vida silvestre, la cultura y el clima, pero necesitan un apoyo global más fuerte para enfrentar las amenazas ambientales crecientes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vast interconnected natural web formed by UNESCO World Heritage sites: misty mountain peaks with a silhouette of a vicuña carrying a bundle of fiber, vibrant coral reef arches beneath the sea, and a sprawling biosphere reserve overlooking a calm Mediterranean coastline, all linked by glowing threads symbolizing protection and harmony between diverse ecosystems and the Indigenous communities who nurture them.

articles.title: One of UK's rarest flies returns to Cairngorms thanks to jam jars
articles.summary: Conservationists say a captive breeding programme has now released 30,000 pine hoverflies into the wild.
articles.content: A rare fly that faced extinction could now outnumber the human population of the Cairngorms National Park - the last place in the UK where the insect survives, say conservationists.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a successful captive breeding program that has released 30,000 rare pine hoverflies back into their natural habitat in the Cairngorms National Park, representing a significant conservation achievement with broad ecological benefits.
articles.personality_title: Rare pine hoverfly makes a strong comeback in Cairngorms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The pine hoverfly is one of the rarest flies in the UK and was close to disappearing completely. It lives only in the Cairngorms National Park, a large protected area in Scotland.

**What happened** – Conservationists started a captive breeding program to raise pine hoverflies safely in jars. They have now released 30,000 of these flies back into the wild in the Cairngorms.

**Impact** – This is important because it helps bring back a species that was nearly extinct. The pine hoverfly plays a role in the local environment, such as helping pollinate plants. Increasing their numbers supports the health of the park’s natural ecosystem.

**What's next step** – Experts will continue to monitor the hoverflies to see how well they survive and grow in the wild. The success of this program could also guide efforts to save other rare insects.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A careful breeding program has helped a rare fly return to its home in the Cairngorms, boosting the park’s natural life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le rare syrphe des pins fait un retour remarquable dans les Cairngorms
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le syrphe des pins est l'une des mouches les plus rares du Royaume-Uni et était proche de disparaître complètement. Il vit uniquement dans le parc national des Cairngorms, une grande zone protégée en Écosse.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des conservateurs ont lancé un programme d'élevage en captivité pour élever les syrphes des pins en toute sécurité dans des bocaux. Ils ont maintenant relâché 30 000 de ces mouches dans la nature dans les Cairngorms.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela aide à ramener une espèce presque éteinte. Le syrphe des pins joue un rôle dans l'environnement local, notamment en aidant à polliniser les plantes. Augmenter leur nombre soutient la santé de l'écosystème naturel du parc.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts continueront de surveiller les syrphes pour voir comment ils survivent et se développent dans la nature. Le succès de ce programme pourrait aussi guider les efforts pour sauver d'autres insectes rares.

**Phrase clé** – Un programme d'élevage soigneux a permis à une mouche rare de revenir dans son habitat naturel des Cairngorms, renforçant la vie naturelle du parc.
articles.personality_title_es: El raro sírfido del pino regresa con fuerza a Cairngorms
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El sírfido del pino es una de las moscas más raras del Reino Unido y estuvo a punto de desaparecer por completo. Solo vive en el Parque Nacional de Cairngorms, una gran área protegida en Escocia.

**Qué pasó** – Los conservacionistas iniciaron un programa de cría en cautiverio para criar sírfidos del pino de forma segura en frascos. Ahora han liberado 30,000 de estas moscas en la naturaleza en Cairngorms.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque ayuda a recuperar una especie que casi se extingue. El sírfido del pino ayuda al ecosistema local, por ejemplo, polinizando plantas. Aumentar su número apoya la salud del ecosistema natural del parque.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos seguirán monitoreando a los sírfidos para ver cómo sobreviven y crecen en la naturaleza. El éxito de este programa podría guiar también esfuerzos para salvar otros insectos raros.

**Frase clave** – Un programa cuidadoso de cría ha ayudado a una mosca rara a regresar a su hogar en Cairngorms, fortaleciendo la vida natural del parque.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a delicate rare fly resting on the rim of a simple, transparent jam jar nestled among gentle Cairngorms mountain landscape silhouettes, with soft natural earth tones and subtle light emphasizing the fly’s fragile beauty and the jar’s role as a protective haven.

articles.title: Scientists just captured trees glowing with electricity during storms
articles.summary: Scientists chasing thunderstorms in a retrofitted minivan finally captured something never seen before in nature: faint electrical glows shimmering from treetops during a storm. These “corona discharges,” long suspected but never observed outside a lab, appeared as tiny UV flashes at the tips of leaves. The discovery could reshape how we understand forests, since these bursts may help clean the air by breaking down pollutants.
articles.content:
In June 2024, a group of Penn State meteorology and atmospheric science researchers set out on a road trip along the East Coast in a modified 2013 Toyota Sienna. The van was outfitted with a custom-built telescopic weather instrument extending from the roof. Their goal was to track down Florida's near-daily summer thunderstorms and observe a phenomenon that had never been confirmed outside a laboratory.

That phenomenon, known as corona discharge, involves tiny bursts of electricity forming at the tips of leaves. These faint electrical pulses can cause treetops to emit a subtle glow in the ultraviolet (UV) range. Scientists have suspected for more than 70 years that forests might produce these effects during storms due to unusual electric field activity, but direct evidence in nature had remained elusive.

A Long-Standing Mystery Finally Tested in the Field

The research team included William Brune, a distinguished professor of meteorology and atmospheric science; Patrick McFarland, a doctoral student in the same field; Jena Jenkins, an assistant research professor; and David Miller, a former associate research professor now working at the Penn State Applied Research Lab. Their objective was to document corona discharges occurring naturally for the first time.

Florida was chosen because of its frequent thunderstorms, which seemed ideal for the study. However, the weather did not cooperate as expected. For three weeks, McFarland and Brune followed short-lived storms that dissipated quickly, leaving them without useful data.

Breakthrough Observation in North Carolina

As the team began heading back to Pennsylvania, conditions changed. Large, long-lasting storms developed just west of Interstate 95. Seizing the opportunity, the researchers stopped at the University of North Carolina at Pembroke and set up their equipment in a parking lot. They aimed their instruments at the upper branches of a sweetgum tree located about 100 feet from their van.

A thunderstorm persisted for nearly two hours, bringing heavy rain and frequent lightning. During this time, the team recorded corona discharges on the sweetgum tree and also observed similar activity on a nearby long needle loblolly pine as the storm weakened. These observations marked the first confirmed detection of corona discharges in a natural setting. The findings were later published in Geophysical Research Letters.

"This just goes to show that there's still discovery science being done," said McFarland, lead author on the paper. "For more than half a century, scientists have theorized that corona exists, but this proves it."

How Corona Discharges Form in Storms

According to the researchers, corona discharges occur because of strong electrical imbalances during storms. Thunderclouds develop large negative charges that attract positive charges on the ground. This positive charge travels upward through trees, concentrating at the highest points such as leaf tips.

At these tiny, hair-like structures, the electric field becomes intense enough to produce a faint glow that can be seen in both visible light and UV. The UV radiation generated by this process can break apart water vapor molecules, leading to the formation of hydroxyl.

Atmospheric Chemistry and Air Cleaning Effects

Hydroxyl plays a key role in the atmosphere as its primary oxidizer. Oxidizers help remove pollutants by reacting with airborne chemicals and transforming them into substances that are easier to eliminate. These reactions involve compounds released by trees as well as human-generated pollutants, including methane, a potent greenhouse gas.

Earlier work by the team showed that corona discharges could be a significant source of these atmospheric cleansing agents within forest canopies. This makes the phenomenon potentially important for air quality and climate processes.

Laboratory Insights and Field Confirmation

The researchers had previously studied this effect in controlled experiments. By applying high-voltage, low-current electrical pulses to tree branches, they found a strong link between UV emissions from corona discharges and the production of hydroxyl. In both those experiments and the recent field observations, they also noted minor damage to leaves at the points where corona formed.

To observe the phenomenon outdoors, the team created the Corona Observing Telescope System. This instrument is a Newtonian telescope connected to a UV-sensitive camera. It includes geolocation capabilities, sensors to measure atmospheric electricity, and calibration using a mercury lamp. The system blocks solar UV wavelengths so that only corona, lightning and fire can produce detectable signals.

Hundreds of Corona Events Captured

Using this system in North Carolina, the team recorded 859 corona events on the sweetgum tree and 93 on the loblolly pine. Each event lasted anywhere from a fraction of a second to several seconds, according to McFarland. Additional observations were made during four other thunderstorms and across four different tree species.

"It's nearly invisible to the naked eye but our instruments give rise to a vision of swaths of scintillating corona glowing as thunderstorms pass overhead," McFarland said. "Such widespread coronae have implications for the removal of hydrocarbons emitted by trees, subtle tree leaf damage and could have broader implications for the health of trees, forests and the atmosphere."

Open Questions About Trees and the Environment

Although the team has confirmed that corona discharges occur in nature, many questions remain. Researchers want to know whether these electrical events harm trees or provide some benefit. They are also investigating whether trees have adapted to tolerate or even take advantage of this process, and whether the resulting atmospheric cleansing benefits forest ecosystems.

To explore these questions, the scientists are beginning collaborations with tree ecologists and biologists. Their work could lead to new insights into how forests interact with the atmosphere and how these interactions influence environmental health.

The study was supported by the U.S. National Science Foundation, with Brune, Jenkins and Miller serving as co-authors.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough with broad environmental implications: the first confirmed observation of corona discharges from trees during storms, which may contribute to atmospheric cleansing by breaking down pollutants. This discovery advances understanding of forest-atmosphere interactions and could impact air quality and climate science, benefiting society at large. The article provides detailed context about the research, methods, and potential environmental significance, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists capture trees glowing with electricity during storms for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long suspected that trees produce tiny electrical glows called corona discharges during thunderstorms. These glows are faint flashes of ultraviolet (UV) light at leaf tips caused by electrical activity, but no one had ever seen them outside a lab.

**What happened** – In June 2024, a team from Penn State used a specially equipped van with a UV telescope to track thunderstorms along the East Coast. After weeks of chasing storms in Florida, they finally observed and recorded these corona discharges on sweetgum and pine trees during a storm in North Carolina. They captured hundreds of tiny UV flashes lasting seconds, confirming the phenomenon in nature for the first time.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because corona discharges produce hydroxyl, a chemical that helps break down pollution in the air. Trees may play a bigger role than we thought in cleaning the atmosphere during storms. The finding also opens new questions about how these electrical bursts affect tree health and forest environments.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to work with tree experts to learn if corona discharges help or harm trees and how this process influences forests and air quality. They hope to better understand these natural electrical events and their role in the environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have for the first time recorded faint electrical glows from tree leaves during storms, revealing a new way forests may help clean the air.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques capturent pour la première fois les arbres brillant d'électricité lors des orages
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les scientifiques pensent que les arbres produisent de petites lueurs électriques appelées décharges corona lors des orages. Ces éclairs UV très faibles apparaissent aux extrémités des feuilles à cause de l’activité électrique, mais personne ne les avait jamais observés en pleine nature.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En juin 2024, une équipe de Penn State a utilisé une camionnette équipée d’un télescope UV pour suivre les orages sur la côte Est des États-Unis. Après plusieurs semaines en Floride, ils ont finalement observé et enregistré ces décharges corona sur des arbres sweetgum et pins en Caroline du Nord. Ils ont capturé des centaines de petits flashs UV durant quelques secondes, confirmant ce phénomène en milieu naturel pour la première fois.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car les décharges corona produisent de l’hydroxyle, une substance qui aide à décomposer la pollution dans l’air. Les arbres pourraient donc jouer un rôle plus grand qu’on le pensait dans le nettoyage de l’atmosphère lors des orages. Cela soulève aussi des questions sur les effets de ces décharges sur la santé des arbres et des forêts.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont collaborer avec des spécialistes des arbres pour savoir si ces décharges aident ou blessent les arbres, et comment elles influencent les forêts et la qualité de l’air. Ils veulent mieux comprendre ce phénomène électrique naturel et son rôle dans l’environnement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des scientifiques ont enregistré les faibles lueurs électriques des feuilles d’arbres lors d’orages, révélant une nouvelle façon dont les forêts pourraient aider à purifier l’air.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos capturan por primera vez árboles brillando con electricidad durante tormentas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han sospechado durante mucho tiempo que los árboles producen pequeños brillos eléctricos llamados descargas corona durante las tormentas. Estos destellos débiles de luz ultravioleta (UV) aparecen en las puntas de las hojas debido a la actividad eléctrica, pero nadie los había visto en la naturaleza.

**Qué pasó** – En junio de 2024, un equipo de Penn State usó una furgoneta especialmente equipada con un telescopio UV para seguir tormentas en la costa Este de Estados Unidos. Tras varias semanas en Florida, finalmente observaron y grabaron estas descargas corona en árboles sweetgum y pino en Carolina del Norte. Capturaron cientos de pequeños destellos UV que duraron segundos, confirmando este fenómeno en la naturaleza por primera vez.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque las descargas corona producen hidroxilo, una sustancia que ayuda a descomponer la contaminación en el aire. Los árboles podrían tener un papel más grande de lo pensado en limpiar la atmósfera durante las tormentas. También abre preguntas sobre cómo estas descargas afectan la salud de los árboles y los bosques.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean trabajar con expertos en árboles para saber si las descargas corona ayudan o dañan a los árboles y cómo este proceso influye en los bosques y la calidad del aire. Quieren entender mejor estos eventos eléctricos naturales y su papel en el medio ambiente.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han registrado por primera vez los débiles brillos eléctricos de las hojas de los árboles durante tormentas, revelando una nueva forma en que los bosques pueden ayudar a limpiar el aire.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing sweetgum tree and a loblolly pine stand under a stormy sky, their leaf tips emitting delicate, shimmering blue-white electric sparks that gently illuminate the surrounding rain and dark clouds, captured in a warm, detailed painting with natural muted greens, blues, and grays.

articles.title: Triple-decker solar cells reach efficiency milestone
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 21 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01149-9A promising solar-cell architecture, in which two layers of perovskite semiconductors are stacked on silicon, is making strides towards achieving its full potential.
articles.content:
The solar-energy revolution has been powered by silicon for decades, but conventional ‘single junction’ solar cells made from crystalline silicon are approaching their practical limits. The best laboratory-scale devices can convert about 28% of sunlight into electricity, close to the maximum efficiency of about 29% that is thought to be possible for cells of this type1. Writing in Nature, Artuk et al.2 report innovations that enable a different solar-cell design — a ‘triple junction’ device in which materials called perovskites are used in combination with silicon — to achieve greater than 30% efficiency. Their work brings such devices closer to fulfilling their long-anticipated potential of reaching efficiencies well beyond those that can be achieved using silicon alone.

doi: https://doi.org/10.1038/d41586-026-01149-9

References Richter, A., Hermle, M., & Glunz, S. W. IEEE J. Photovolt. 3, 1184–1191 (2013). Artuk, K. et al. Nature https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-026-10385-y (2026). Chapin, D. M., Fuller, C. S. & Pearson, G. L. J. Appl. Phys. 25, 676–677 (1954). Green, M. A. Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 17, 183–189 (2009). Green, M. A. et al. Joule 10, 102240 (2026). Kojima, A., Teshima, K., Shirai, Y. & Miyasaka, T. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 131, 6050–6051 (2009). Restat, L. et al. Sol. RRL 8, 2300887 (2024). Yao, Y. et al. Nature Rev. Clean Technol. 1, 771–787 (2025). Duan, L. et al. Nature Rev. Mater. 8, 261–281 (2023). Download references

Competing Interests S.D.S. is a co-founder of Swift Solar, Inc.

Related Articles

Subjects
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in solar cell efficiency, surpassing previous limits of silicon-based cells by using a triple-junction design with perovskite layers. This innovation has broad implications for renewable energy generation and climate action, potentially enabling more efficient and widespread solar power adoption, which benefits society and the environment on a large scale.
articles.personality_title: New triple-layer solar cells surpass efficiency limits of silicon alone
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, solar panels have mostly used silicon to turn sunlight into electricity. These silicon solar cells are getting close to their highest possible efficiency, about 29%, meaning they convert almost 3 out of every 10 sunlight particles into usable power.

**What happened** – Scientists have built a new type of solar cell that stacks two layers of a material called perovskite on top of silicon, creating a 'triple-junction' cell. This new design has reached more than 30% efficiency, beating the best silicon-only cells. The discovery was reported by Artuk and colleagues in the journal Nature in April 2026.

**Impact** – This achievement is important because it shows that solar cells can be made more efficient by combining materials. Higher efficiency means solar panels can produce more electricity from the same amount of sunlight. This could help solar energy become more affordable and common, making better use of space and resources.

**What's next step** – Researchers will work on improving the durability and manufacturing processes of these triple-junction cells. If successful, these advanced solar panels could soon be produced on a large scale and used in homes, businesses, and solar farms.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have developed solar cells with three layers that convert sunlight into electricity more efficiently than traditional silicon-only cells, opening the door to better solar power technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles cellules solaires à triple couche dépassent les limites d'efficacité du silicium seul
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des années, les panneaux solaires utilisent principalement le silicium pour transformer la lumière du soleil en électricité. Ces cellules solaires en silicium approchent de leur efficacité maximale, environ 29 %, ce qui signifie qu'elles convertissent près de 3 particules de lumière sur 10 en énergie utilisable.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont créé un nouveau type de cellule solaire qui empile deux couches d'un matériau appelé pérovskite sur du silicium, formant une cellule à « triple jonction ». Ce nouveau design a atteint une efficacité de plus de 30 %, dépassant les meilleures cellules à base de silicium uniquement. Cette découverte a été rapportée par Artuk et ses collègues dans la revue Nature en avril 2026.

**Impact** – Cette avancée est importante car elle montre que les cellules solaires peuvent être rendues plus efficaces en combinant des matériaux. Une meilleure efficacité signifie que les panneaux solaires produisent plus d'électricité avec la même quantité de lumière. Cela pourrait rendre l'énergie solaire plus abordable et plus répandue, optimisant l'espace et les ressources.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont travailler à améliorer la durabilité et les méthodes de fabrication de ces cellules à triple jonction. Si cela réussit, ces panneaux solaires avancés pourraient bientôt être produits en grande quantité et utilisés dans les maisons, les entreprises et les fermes solaires.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont développé des cellules solaires à trois couches qui convertissent la lumière du soleil en électricité plus efficacement que les cellules traditionnelles en silicium, ouvrant la voie à une meilleure technologie solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas células solares de triple capa superan los límites de eficiencia del silicio solo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, los paneles solares han usado principalmente silicio para convertir la luz solar en electricidad. Estas células solares de silicio están cerca de su eficiencia máxima, alrededor del 29 %, lo que significa que convierten casi 3 de cada 10 partículas de luz en energía útil.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos han creado un nuevo tipo de célula solar que apila dos capas de un material llamado perovskita sobre silicio, formando una célula de 'triple unión'. Este nuevo diseño alcanzó más del 30 % de eficiencia, superando las mejores células solo de silicio. El descubrimiento fue publicado por Artuk y sus colegas en la revista Nature en abril de 2026.

**Impacto** – Este logro es importante porque demuestra que las células solares pueden ser más eficientes al combinar materiales. Mayor eficiencia significa que los paneles solares pueden producir más electricidad con la misma cantidad de luz. Esto podría ayudar a que la energía solar sea más barata y común, usando mejor el espacio y los recursos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores trabajarán en mejorar la durabilidad y los procesos de fabricación de estas células de triple unión. Si tienen éxito, estos paneles solares avanzados podrían producirse a gran escala y usarse en hogares, negocios y plantas solares.

**Frase clave** – Científicos desarrollaron células solares con tres capas que convierten la luz solar en electricidad más eficientemente que las células tradicionales solo de silicio, abriendo paso a mejor tecnología solar.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of three layered, translucent solar cells stacked like delicate glass panels glowing softly with natural light, each layer subtly textured to represent silicon and perovskite materials, set against a serene sky at dawn with gentle golden and soft blue hues, symbolizing breakthrough efficiency and harmony between advanced technology and nature.
